C[komi: FYI, Amazon have announced full-time back to the office
C[RedLeopard: Interesting -- it will be a global trend.
RedLeopard: Started with Goldman Sachs very soon after lockdowns were over
C[RedLeopard: Corporate control trumps convenience and productivity. (Although jury is out on productivity with remote work)
RedLeopard: When will you be in Cape Town again?
komi: It is ludicrously hard to measure productivity in knowledge industries.
komi: Some metrics are clearly better with remote work, e.g. (theoretical) hours of work.
komi: other metrics are much fuzzier, e.g. focus (less interruptions), collaboration (asking for help vs. getting stuck)
C[RedLeopard: yes, very interesting about the metrics -- and accurate metrics might not be perceived as fair or not feasible to implement
C[komi: I will be in CPT mid Jan to mid Feb again
C[RedLeopard: The Amazon decision also made it onto the Hard Fork podcast of the NYT
C[RedLeopard: whoops. why did we put time-controls?

jaedge:  perhaps the writer has an overblown sense of his own judgements

jaedge: It is true that students I have encountered so far generally do not often show the assertiveness, or forwardness, in class that students in Western unis exult in. But there is no doubt of exceptional intelligence and perceptiveness in most.

jaedge: I am sceptical of his argument about how phonetic scripts aid learning outcomes. Seems like a thumbsuck. It was hardly a generation or two ago when most students had to learn Latin and Greek as well as modern languages to enter college. More complex language learning demands, if anything it heightened awareness of the the kind of things asociated with detailed procesing of information.
jaedge: My guess would be that Spaniards, and other Europeans, demonstrate higher literacy performance because there schools and teachers are in better shape than the average English speaking system.
jaedge: The degree of dumbing down in English over the last 20 years is something to behold.

jaedge: Novels by authors like Austin and Dickens are hardle read even at high school, often abridged. Students learn to debate about the relevance of Shakespeare without ever having read or seen a play, let alone having immersed themselves in the study of one.
